tasks.instruction: Przeczytaj ponizszy tekst. Zdecyduj, ktore zdania (1.1.–1.6.) sa zgodne z trescia tekstu (T – True), ktore sa niezgodne (F – False), a ktore zawieraja informacje niepodane w tekscie (NI – No Information). Nastepnie znajdz w tekscie wyrazy lub wyrazenia, ktore odpowiadaja definicjom podanym w punktach 1.7.–1.11. Wymagana jest calkowita poprawnosc ortograficzna odpowiedzi.
tasks.text:
A YEAR VOLUNTEERING ABROAD

When I finished school at eighteen, I was not ready to start at university straight away. My parents expected me to apply at once, but I felt that I needed a break and a real adventure first. After weeks of searching online, I signed up with a small charity that sends young people overseas to help in rural schools. Three months later, I was on a plane to Tanzania, more nervous than I had ever been in my life.

The village where I was placed had no internet and only a few hours of electricity each evening. At first this frightened me, but I soon got used to it. My main job was to assist a local teacher with English lessons for children aged six to eleven. I had imagined that I would simply stand at the front and talk, yet teaching turned out to be far harder than I expected. The pupils were eager but shy, and it took me a month to win their trust.

Life in the village was not always easy. The rainy season turned the roads to thick mud, and once I was ill for a week with a stomach bug. Still, the kindness of the families I lived with made up for every difficulty. They shared their meals with me even when food was scarce, and they taught me a few words of Swahili every day.

Looking back, that year was the turning point of my life. I learned that I am more independent than I believed, and I discovered that I genuinely love teaching. I am now training to become a primary school teacher in Britain, and I still write letters to two of my former pupils. Volunteering abroad did not make me rich, but it gave me something far more valuable: a clearer idea of who I want to be.

items.statement: The narrator decided to postpone going to university.
items.explanation: TRUE — the narrator 'was not ready to start at university straight away' and 'felt that I needed a break and a real adventure first'.

items.statement: The narrator's parents encouraged the idea of volunteering abroad instead of studying.
items.explanation: FALSE — the parents 'expected me to apply at once', which contradicts encouraging a year abroad first.

items.statement: The village where the narrator stayed had a reliable internet connection.
items.explanation: FALSE — the village 'had no internet and only a few hours of electricity each evening'.

items.statement: Teaching proved more difficult than the narrator had expected.
items.explanation: TRUE — 'teaching turned out to be far harder than I expected'.

items.statement: The narrator paid the host families for the meals they provided.
items.explanation: NO INFORMATION — the text says the families 'shared their meals with me even when food was scarce' but never states whether any payment was made.

items.statement: After the year abroad, the narrator gave up the idea of becoming a teacher.
items.explanation: FALSE — the narrator is 'now training to become a primary school teacher in Britain'.

vocabItems.definition: an organisation that helps people in need and is not run to make a profit
in or to a foreign country, across the sea
keen and full of enthusiasm to do something
available only in small amounts; not enough to meet demand
a moment when an important change begins to happen
